text: The surgeon maintains the urechial attachment to keep the bladder tethered to the umbilicus, dividing the peritoneum down to a pre-marked point where the median umbilical ligament crosses the vas deferens. The pubic arch bone is cleared with a sweeping movement, and the same maneuver is performed on the opposite side, dividing the peritoneum to the pre-marked point. The retzius space is partially opened towards the midline to connect with the space on the contralateral side.

The surgeon releases some proximal fibers tethered to the umbilicus to create space, using the fourth robotic arm to retract the bladder down and right. This maneuver creates a window for the camera and assistants to advance towards the pelvis. The endopelvic fascia is preserved, and the anterior bladder neck is divided directly using a thirty-degree down scope, without prior opening of the endopelvic fascia.

The surgeon performs a unilateral opening of the endopelvic fascia on the left side, leaving the right side closed to maintain pelvic floor tension. The curvature from the open endopelvic fascia on the left aids in defining the anterior base of the prostate. The anterior bladder neck dissection proceeds in the usual manner.

The surgeon undertakes nerve sparing in a modified fashion due to the closed endopelvic fascia. Hemlock clips are applied to release the dorsolateral vascular pedicle at the base of the prostate. The nerve sparing plane is entered anteriorly at the prostate base, with the fourth arm rotating the prostate out of the pelvis for better access. The nerve sparing continues in an antegrade fashion, with the option to approach retrograde, using hemlock clips to bridge the gap on the dorsolateral pedicle.

The surgeon corrects the dissection from the interfacial to the intrafacial plane, peeling away a closer layer of tissue. The dissection continues in the intrafacial plane towards the anterior fibromuscular tissue, releasing the anterior apron from the base of the prostate. Monopolar diathermy is used safely due to the distance from the neurovascular bundle, and the procedure is repeated on the opposite side.

The surgeon approaches the apex of the prostate, retracting it dorsally and cranially using either the left arm or the fourth arm. Heat is applied to hug the anterior fibromuscular surface of the prostate, aiming for minimal opening of the DVC vessels. Alternatively, scissors are used for cold cutting along the underside of the DVC, again minimizing vessel opening. The apex of the prostate is carefully delineated, aided by a clean ventral lateral dissection on the right side.

The surgeon proceeds towards the urethra at the apex of the prostate, aiming to preserve maximal urethral length. The junction between the prostate and urethra is clearly defined, with the catheter in place. The urethra is sharply divided, revealing the verumontanum within the prosthetic urethra, highlighting the stable urethral length achieved with this anterioretsia sparing approach.

The surgeon completes the urethral dissection and removes the prostate, placing it in an endocatch bag. The prostatic bed appears different post-removal, resembling a shell without train tracks. The DVC is visible but does not require oversewing. Benign tissue at the apical margin is sent for analysis due to concerns.
